C
Where Ip represents the peak current, A denotes the electrode area, D is the diffusion coefficient, n is the number of electrons involved in the reaction (equal to 1), v indicates the scan rate, and C is the concentration of the probe molecule (4 mM). CV analysis suggested that the oxidation states of silver influenced anodic peaks associated with TC oxidation. The Ag/GO/BDD electrode exhibited linear increases in peak currents with TC concentration, demonstrating its effectiveness for TC detection across concentrations ranging from 10 to 50 µM. Moreover, the effect of the scan rate showed that the anodic current was directly proportional to the square root of the scan rate, indicating a diffusion-controlled process [184].

An innovative sensor was developed for detecting oxytetracycline (OTC) in milk, utilizing a GCE modified with a tantalum pentoxide nanoparticle-electrochemically reduced graphene oxide (Ta2O5-ErGO) nanocomposite. The electrochemical performance of the sensor was assessed using CV. The cyclic voltammograms obtained for various electrodes in the presence of K3[Fe(CN)6] revealed distinct redox behavior, as illustrated in Fig. 9
a. The results indicated that Ta2O5-ErGO/GCE exhibited the highest anodic peak current (ipa = 135.7 µA), nearly double that of the bare GCE (60.52 µA). The electrochemical active area of Ta2O5-ErGO/GCE was approximately twice that of the bare GCE, significantly enhancing OTC's adsorption and redox reaction on the electrode surface. The oxidation of OTC was determined to be an irreversible process, with the Ta2O5-ErGO/GCE significantly outperforming other electrode configurations, including bare GCE, GO/GCE, and ErGO/GCE. The pH was systematically investigated for optimal detection conditions, revealing that a pH of 6.5 yielded the highest peak current. The CV responses of 10 μM OTC in 0.1 M PBS at different electrodes showed the distinct electrochemical behavior of OTC, as depicted in Fig. 9 b. The sensor demonstrated high selectivity, showing minimal interference from potential contaminants, including metal ions and other antibiotics. Reproducibility was confirmed with an RSD of 2.34 %, indicating consistent performance across multiple electrodes. Additionally, the sensor maintained stability over time, with only a 13 % decrease in signal observed after 25 cycles over five days. The calibration curve established a linear relationship between peak current and OTC concentration in the range of 0.2 to 100 µM, with a detection limit of 0.095 µM [185].

Sulym et al. designed an innovative electrochemical sensor for the selective and rapid detection of TC by incorporating L-histidine functionalized multi-walled carbon nanotubes (L-His-MWCNTs) into a molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP). The sensor was constructed on a GCE by modifying the surface with L-His-MWCNTs and employing a sol–gel polymerization process. The composite was synthesized using tetraethoxysilane (TEOS) and cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) in the presence of TC, forming specific cavities within the polymer that facilitate selective binding of TC. Electrochemical performance was evaluated through CV, DPV, and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). The sensor exhibited a very low LOD of 2.642 × 10−12 M and a linear detection range between 1 × 10−11 M and 1 × 10−1⁰ M, indicating excellent sensitivity. Optimization of various parameters, such as the amount of L-His-MWCNTs@PDMS-5 and the volume of solution applied to the electrode, further improved the sensor's stability and performance. The sensor was validated with real sample testing in capsule dosage forms, human serum, and tap water. The recovery rates were remarkably high, with 99.69 % for capsules, 98.92 % for serum, and 100.60 % for tap water. These results demonstrate that the developed L-His-MWCNTs@PDMS-5/MIP sensor is highly effective for detecting TC, making it a valuable tool for pharmaceutical, environmental, and clinical applications where accurate and sensitive detection of tetracycline is required [186].

Amaral et al. developed a novel electrochemical sensor using a GCE modified with MWCNT and a Fe3O4@SiO2 nanocomposite for detecting TC. The composite material enhanced the sensor's electrochemical performance and stability due to the synergistic properties of MWCNTs, such as high electrical conductivity and large surface area. Regarding sensor configuration, the GCE/MWCNT/Fe3O4@SiO2 configuration exhibited clear anodic and cathodic peaks for TC detection, showing superior stability and electrochemical activity compared to other configurations. In particular, the GCE/MWCNT/Fe3O4@SiO2 exhibited an anodic peak current at + 0.52 V and a cathodic peak current at + 0.50 V. Employing AdSDPV, the sensor showed a linear response for TC concentrations ranging from 4.0 to 36 µmol L−1 (R2 = 0.997) and from 40 to 64 µmol L−1 (R2 = 0.994). The LOD and limit of quantification (LOQ) were 1.67 µmol L−1 and 4.0 µmol L−1, respectively. The electroactive area increased significantly from 0.089 cm2 (bare GCE) to 0.158 cm2 (composite), attributed to effective stabilization and enhanced electron transfer. This increase was confirmed using the Randles–Sevcik equation, highlighting the modified electrode's elevated electroactive area compared to the bare GCE. Furthermore, the electrochemical process was identified as adsorption-controlled, as demonstrated by the analysis of current response at different scan rates. This is illustrated in Fig. 10 b, where the plot of log Ipa​ (anodic peak current) versus log ν (scan rate) produced a straight line with a slope of 0.94. Optimization of experimental parameters revealed that the best concentrations for MWCNT and Fe3O4@SiO2 were 2.50 mg mL−1, as illustrated in Fig. 10 c and 10d, where significant increases in current were observed at these concentrations. The optimal pH value for the sensor was determined to be pH 3, as shown in Fig. 10 e, which provided the highest anodic current intensity due to the favorable protonation of TC in an acidic medium. The study of the support electrolyte influence (Fig. 10 f) indicated that hydrochloric acid (HCl) significantly increased the analytical signal without any detectable interaction with TC. The sensor exhibited excellent reproducibility, with an RSD of −0.71 %, and demonstrated selectivity against interfering substances such as doxycycline and diclofenac, allowing for accurate TC detection in complex mixtures. The sensor was successfully applied to real-world samples, including milk and river water, yielding 91 % and 117 % recovery values. These results confirmed the sensor's reliability and highlighted its ability to perform well without matrix effects. In conclusion, the GCE/MWCNT/Fe33O4@SiO2 sensor represents a promising tool for the sensitive, stable, and selective detection of tetracycline in various samples, offering significant advantages over existing methods [187].

In another study, Guo et al. studied a voltammetric method for detecting TC using an MWNT film coated on a GCE, enhanced with the ionic liquid 1-octyl-3-methylimidazolium hexafluorophosphate (OMIMPF6). The technique shows significant sensitivity, with a notable anodic peak observed at 0.54 V (vs. SCE) in a phosphate buffer at pH 7.0. The OMIMPF6-MWNT/GCE outperformed other electrodes, showing improved oxidation of TC due to enhanced MWNT dispersion and increased effective surface area. Optimization of conditions revealed that the optimal pH for detection is 7.0, as the peak current decreases below pH 5.0. Additionally, the anodic peak current correlates linearly with the scan rate, which was tested in the range of 0.003 to 0.6 V/s, indicating that the electrochemical process is controlled by adsorption. A linear relationship between peak current and TC concentration was established, ranging from 1.1 × 10-7 M to 2.2 × 10-5 M, with a detection limit of 3 × 10-8 M. The method demonstrated excellent repeatability, with an RSD of approximately 2 %, and reproducibility, with an RSD of less than 7 %. Furthermore, the technique was successfully applied to determine TC in pharmaceutical formulations and egg samples, yielding 91 % and 105 % recoveries, confirming its reliability. In conclusion, the OMIMPF6-MWNT/GCE method presents a sensitive and reliable approach for monitoring tetracycline in various samples, making it suitable for environmental and food safety applications [188].

Another work highlights using N-CQDs synthesized from Ruta graveolens leaves through a green hydrothermal method for detecting TC, a common antibiotic. The N-CQDs exhibited an 18 % quantum yield (QY) and were characterized using various techniques. Their surfaces are rich in hydroxyl, carbonyl, and amino groups, enhancing solubility, stability, and sensing ability. Zeta potential analysis indicated a negatively charged surface of − 26.5 mV, contributing to their stability and reactivity. The N-CQDs function as effective photoluminescent (PL) and electrochemical sensors for TC. The PL sensor showed a linear range of 0–600 nM with an LOD of 60 nM, while the electrochemical sensor demonstrated a linear range of 0–30 nM and an LOD of 0.28 nM. Stability studies indicated that PL intensity remained stable across a pH range of 3 to 12, with minimal effects from ionic strength, confirming the salt resistance of N-CQDs. Samples stored at 4 °C for three months showed negligible changes in PL intensity, and their PL remained unaffected during continuous irradiation for 2 h, indicating high photostability. To optimize the sensing process, the optimal pH for detection was determined to be 7, with maximum fluorescence quenching efficiency observed after a 4-minute interaction time with TC. Interference studies demonstrated that the N-CQD sensor retained high specificity for TC, even in the presence of other antibiotics at concentrations of 600 nM. Additionally, modified GCE with N-CQDs showed enhanced electroactive surface area and improved electron transfer, with the electrochemical oxidation of TC being optimal at pH 4. The practical applicability of the N-CQD sensor was further demonstrated by its successful use in detecting TC in real water samples, including tap and river water. Recovery rates for TC detection ranged from 98.63 % to 100.88 %, with an RSD of 0.18–0.59 % in PL analysis and RSD values of 0.75 % to 1.30 % via the DPV method. Overall, N-CQDs from R. graveolens present a promising avenue for sensitive and selective electrochemical detection of tetracycline, with potential applications in environmental monitoring and clinical settings. Future research could expand their use to detect other pharmaceutical contaminants [189].

The study of Zhang et al. presents a novel fluorescence-based sensing method utilizing NCDs incorporated into metal–organic coordination polymers (MOCPs), specifically (4-Hap)4(Mo8O26). The NCDs@(4-Hap)4(Mo8O26) composite exhibits bright blue fluorescence with a peak at 465 nm under UV excitation (380 nm), demonstrating stable luminescence properties. Notably, the fluorescence shows a redshift of 20 nm upon integration into the MOCP, indicating effective interaction between the components. The presence of TC causes significant fluorescence quenching, achieving a quenching efficiency of 94.1 % at 166 µM TC primarily through the inner filter effect (IFE). The sensor demonstrates a linear response for TC concentrations ranging from 8 to 107 µM, with a LOD of 33.9 nM. This high sensitivity is crucial for accurate detection in complex water environments. The kinetics of the response are illustrated in Fig. 11
a, which shows that fluorescence is quenched within 10 s of TC addition, reaching equilibrium quickly. Further experiments, as depicted in Fig. 11 b, demonstrate that as TC concentration increases, fluorescence quenching intensifies, with a notable 85.7 % quenching observed at 300 µL of a 1 mM TC solution and further increasing to 94.1 % with 500 µL addition. The quantitative relationship between the fluorescence emission and TC concentration is summarized in Fig. 11 c, where a linear correlation is established with a coefficient (R2) of 0.9903 over the concentration range of 8–107 µM, demonstrating the effectiveness of the sensor. Moreover, the NCDs@(4-Hap)4(Mo8O26) sensor exhibits high selectivity for TC, with minimal interference from other common ions and antibiotics. The robustness of the sensor is confirmed by consistent quenching efficiency, even in the presence of competing substances. Mechanistic investigations reveal that the structural integrity of the composite is maintained after exposure to TC, ruling out structural collapse as a cause of quenching. Fluorescence lifetime studies indicate that static quenching, rather than resonance energy transfer, is the primary mechanism responsible for the observed fluorescence quenching. The overlap of TC’s absorption spectrum with the excitation spectrum of NCDs@(4-Hap)4(Mo8O26) leads to IFE-induced quenching, enhancing selectivity and response time. The practical applicability of this sensing platform is validated through testing in real water samples, including tap and river water, where recovery rates for TC detection range from 91.95 % to 104.72 %. These results confirm the reliability and accuracy of the NCDs@(4-Hap)4(Mo8O26) composite for environmental monitoring. In conclusion, this work highlights the potential of using NCDs@(4-Hap)4(Mo8O26) as an effective and efficient tool for the electrochemical detection of tetracycline in water, paving the way for future research to enhance sensor capabilities and broaden their application to other environmental contaminants. The practical applicability of this sensing platform is validated through testing in real water samples, including tap and river water, where recovery rates for TC detection range from 91.95 % to 104.72 %. These results confirm the reliability and accuracy of the NCDs@(4-Hap)4(Mo8O26) composite for environmental monitoring. In conclusion, this work highlights the potential of using NCDs@(4-Hap)4(Mo8O26) as an effective and efficient tool for the electrochemical detection of tetracycline in water, paving the way for future research to enhance sensor capabilities and broaden their application to other environmental contaminants [190].

The synthesized CDs can detect TC through fluorescence quenching via the Inner Filter Effect (IFE). A strong linear relationship exists between the fluorescence intensity ratio (F0/F) and TC concentration within the range of 10.0 to 400.0 μM, with the linear equation given by F0/F = 0.00619ctas + 0.9234 and a high correlation coefficient of R2 = 0.9966. The detection limit is 6.0 μM, indicating the method’s high sensitivity for practical applications. Moreover, the detection method exhibits remarkable selectivity for TC, as minimal interference is observed from common excipients found in TC tablets, such as starch, talcum powder, dextrin, glucose, and magnesium stearate, which showed fluorescence change ratios below 10 %. The method was successfully employed to quantify TC in commercial tablets, yielding an average TC content of 0.2480 g (n = 5, RSD = 2.6 %), consistent with high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) results of 0.2445 g. This agreement verifies the accuracy of the fluorescence-based method. Investigations into the mechanistic aspects of the detection revealed that the quenching mechanism is predominantly static. The fluorescence lifetime of the CDs is measured at 6.38 ns, and spectral analyses indicate no significant changes in the CDs' absorption characteristics after the addition of TC, reinforcing the conclusion that the IFE is the primary mechanism facilitating TC detection [191].

Table 2 is a comparative summary of various electrochemical sensors for detecting TC using nanocomposite materials, organized in a table format. The key parameters, including sensor type, LOD, linear range, and recovery percentage, are summarized based on the information provided.

As mentioned, the increasing presence of TC and other antibiotics in water sources poses significant environmental and health challenges. Carbon nanomaterials, such as GO, CNTs, and CDs, have emerged as promising materials for the removal, photocatalytic degradation, and electrochemical detection of tetracycline. However, several challenges hinder their practical application. The synthesis and functionalization of these materials can be complex. For instance, while GO can be synthesized through harsh chemical methods that may introduce defects affecting its properties, achieving uniform functionalization for targeted applications is challenging. Similarly, producing high-quality CNTs is challenging due to aggregation and the need for precise control over the growth process, complicating functionalization to enhance interactions with tetracycline. Moreover, synthesizing CDs can lead to inconsistencies in size and surface properties, which impact their photocatalytic and detection efficiencies. In addition to synthesis challenges, the stability and reusability of carbon nanomaterials under various environmental conditions are crucial for practical applications. Their tendency to aggregate can hinder their effectiveness in real-world scenarios, such as wastewater treatment. Ensuring the reusability of these materials in degradation and detection applications while maintaining their performance is a significant concern. Furthermore, the potential toxicity of carbon nanomaterials to aquatic organisms and humans must be addressed, as understanding their environmental fate and toxicity is critical for their safe application. Lastly, while carbon nanomaterials promise to remove or degrade tetracycline, their efficiency can vary widely based on environmental conditions, including pH, temperature, and other contaminants. Looking ahead, advanced synthesis techniques will play a pivotal role in overcoming these challenges. Developing greener synthesis methods that minimize toxic byproducts while enhancing the uniformity and functionalization of carbon nanomaterials is essential. Electrochemical reduction and microwave-assisted synthesis present promising avenues for future research. Additionally, exploring hybrid materials—combining carbon nanomaterials with other materials, such as metal oxides, polymers, or nanocomposites—can enhance their photocatalytic efficiency and adsorption capacity. Hybrid systems can be engineered to specifically target pollutants, including tetracycline, improving their overall effectiveness.

There is also significant potential for improving photocatalytic systems utilizing GO, CNTs, and CDs under visible light to enhance the degradation of tetracycline. Research into doping these materials with metals, such as TiO2 or ZnO, could increase their photocatalytic activity and stability. Moreover, advancements in electrochemical detection using carbon nanomaterials for real-time monitoring of tetracycline levels show promise. Utilizing molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) with carbon nanomaterials can enhance specificity and sensitivity, leading to more effective detection methods. Integrating carbon nanomaterials with smart sensors and IoT technology can facilitate continuous monitoring of tetracycline levels in water sources, thereby improving environmental management and safety. Addressing regulatory and safety considerations is also essential as the application of carbon nanomaterials expands. Establishing guidelines and regulations for their safe use is crucial, necessitating research to understand their long-term environmental impacts. Collaboration across disciplines—combining chemists, environmental scientists, and engineers—will drive innovation in applying carbon nanomaterials for tetracycline removal and detection. Interdisciplinary approaches can yield holistic solutions to combat antibiotic pollution. Additionally, future studies should focus on scaling carbon nanomaterials in real-world scenarios, such as wastewater treatment plants, to demonstrate their effectiveness and practicality for tetracycline removal. Finally, emphasizing sustainability in producing and applying carbon nanomaterials will be vital for long-term success. Developing biodegradable or recyclable nanomaterials may become a key focus area for researchers. In conclusion, carbon nanomaterials, particularly graphene oxide, carbon nanotubes, and carbon dots, present exciting opportunities for addressing the challenges posed by tetracycline contamination in the environment. Overcoming synthesis challenges, ensuring environmental safety, and advancing research in hybrid materials and electrochemical detection will be key to their successful application in water purification and pollution monitoring. Future developments in this field could lead to innovative and sustainable solutions for one of today's pressing environmental issues.

In conclusion, the recent advancements in CNMs represent a significant leap forward in addressing the challenge es posed by TC pollution in the environment. This review highlights the multifaceted roles of CNMs, including their potential in the removal, photodegradation, and electrochemical detection of tetracycline. Unlike previous studies, which often focus on one aspect of CNMs' capabilities, this work emphasizes the unique structural properties and types of them such as graphene oxide, carbon nanotubes, and carbon quantum dots. These nanomaterials exhibit exceptional performance in adsorbing TC, facilitating photocatalytic degradation, and enabling sensitive electrochemical sensing, offering innovative and holistic strategies for mitigating antibiotic contamination in water sources.

While the findings are promising, several challenges remain, including the optimization of material synthesis, scaling up production processes, and ensuring the long-term stability and reusability of CNMs. Moreover, compared to earlier studies, this review calls for a more thorough evaluation of the potential environmental impacts of CNMs to ensure their safe integration into existing water treatment systems. Future research should focus on enhancing CNM efficiency through material modifications, hybridization, and the development of novel composite materials. The exploration of synergistic effects with other pollutants and the integration of CNM-based technologies into comprehensive environmental monitoring systems will be essential in overcoming these challenges. Overall, the utilization of carbon nanomaterials presents a promising and unique pathway toward sustainable solutions for antibiotic contamination, underscoring their vital role in promoting environmental health and safety.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Jameel M.A. Sulaiman is an esteemed academic in College of Alnoor University. His research focuses on advancing the fields of nanocomposite materials, biomaterials, and cell-semiconductor interfaces.

Farag M.A. Altalbawy is an accomplished researcher in the Department of Chemistry at University College of Duba, University of Tabuk. His work encompasses various areas within chemistry, where he aims to foster scientific discovery and innovation that contributes to both academic and practical applications in the field.

Anjan Kumar is an Assistant Professor in the Department of Electronics and Communication Engineering at GLA University in Mathura, India. He holds a Ph.D. from the National Institute of Technology in Patna and completed his M.Tech in VLSI Design at the Indian Institute of Information Technology in Gwalior. His academic journey commenced with a B.Tech in Electronics and Communication Engineering from Cochin University of Science and Technology. Dr. Kumar has authored numerous research papers in leading journals and presented at various national and international conferences. He actively engages students through initiatives that expose them to recent advancements in technology and industry trends.

Prakash Kanjariya is a committed researcher at the Marwadi University Research Center, where he works in the Department of Physics within the Faculty of Science. His work encompasses a variety of foundational topics, contributing to the advancement of knowledge in Interdisciplinary research.

M.M. Rekha is an Assistant Professor in the Department of Chemistry and Biochemistry at the School of Sciences, JAIN (Deemed to be University). With a focus on integrating chemistry and biochemistry in her research and teaching, she aims to inspire students and foster a deep understanding of scientific principles.

G.V. Siva Prasad is a part of the Department of Chemistry at Raghu Engineering College. His research primarily revolves around organic synthesis, where he explores innovative reactions and compounds. Dr. Siva Prasad is dedicated to both teaching and research, striving to deepen student engagement with chemistry.

Dukhbhanjan Singh is a researcher at the Chitkara University Institute of Engineering and Technology. His focus lies in Interdisciplinary research, where he works on initiatives that enhance academic knowledge and practical applications within the field.

Rafid Jihad Albadr is affiliated with Ahl al Bayt University in Iraq. He is dedicated to advancing education and research in his specialized areas, contributing to the development of his institution and the academic community in Iraq.

Shaxnoza Saydaxmetova serves in the Department of Chemistry and Its Teaching Methods at Tashkent State Pedagogical University in Uzbekistan. She focuses on enhancing educational methodologies in chemistry, aiming to equip future educators with effective teaching strategies.

Pushpa Negi Bhakuni is an Associate Professor in the Department of Allied Science at Graphic Era Hill University. She also holds an adjunct position at Graphic Era Deemed to be University. Her research areas include nanomaterials and environmental pollutants, where she investigates innovative solutions to pressing environmental challenges.

Ali Hussein Athab works in the Al-Zahrawi University College in Iraq. He is committed to enhancing education and practice through his teaching and research efforts, contributing significantly to the Interdisciplinary research.

Aseel Salah Mansoor is an academic at Gilgamesh Ahliya University in Baghdad, Iraq. He focuses on his educational and research responsibilities, with the aim of fostering a rich learning environment that encourages student engagement and scientific inquiry.

Usama Kadem Radi is a faculty member at the National University of Science and Technology in Iraq. His research work focuses on Interdisciplinary researchو where he contributes to advancements in the field through education and collaboration with peers.

Nasr Saadoun Abd is affiliated with the at Al-Farahidi University in Iraq. He is dedicated to Interdisciplinary research professionals, ensuring that students acquire the necessary skills for success in the these field.

Khursheed Muzammil is an Associate Professor in the Department of Public Health at the College of Applied Medical Sciences, King Khalid University, Khamis Mushait Campus, Saudi Arabia. His research focuses on public health issues, aiming to improve community health outcomes through preventive measures and health education. Dr. Muzammil is committed to advancing public health knowledge and practice by Interdisciplinary research.
